coredata.dc:title: Strategy to synthesize long-wavelength emission carbon dots and their multifunctional application for pH variation and arginine sensing and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Yating
Guo, Qiaozhi
Xu, Hongmei
Jiao, Yuan
Liu, Yang
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we designed N and S co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) with long-wavelength emission and their multifunctional application in pH variation, arginine (Arg) sensing, bioimaging in living cells and zebrafish, and fluorescent materials. The N,S-CDs with excitation wavelength-dependent properties were prepared using neutral red (NR) and dl-methionine (DL-Met) as raw materials by one-pot hydrothermal strategy. The N,S-CDs exhibited a unique pH-sensitive luminescence trait within pH range of 3.2–11.0 and have great linear relationship of 4.8–8.0, which indicating their potential application as an imaging reagent in physiological environments. Arg can quench the PL of N,S-CDs due to static quenching. (SQ). The linear range is 2.5–62.5 μM and the LOD is calculated as 0.68 μM. Furthermore, the as-proposed N,S-CDs can be applied as imaging reagents for monitoring of pH and Arg in vivo and vitro owing to outstanding biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Interestingly, the N,S-CDs were also used in fluorescent composite films and phosphors owing to exceptional optical properties. All these results indicate that the N,S-CDs have huge potentiality in the areas of fluorescence sensing, bioimaging and fluorescent materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Orange emission
pH variation
Arginine sensing
Bioimaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087 , Grants 22104075 ), the Graduate Student Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (No. 2021Y036 ), the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province, China (No. 20210302124014 ) .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and dimensional distribution diagram (bottom inset); (B) FTIR; (C) XPS survey and (D) C1s, (E) N1s and (F) S2p of N,S-CDs.

(A) UV–Vis and photoluminescence spectrum; (B) Excitation-dependent PL performance; (C) Chromaticity color coordinates of N,S-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence titration curve of pH (3.2–11.0); (B) The relationship between the emission intensity and pH; (C) The linear relationship between PL intensity and pH; (D) Proposed sensing mechanism of N,S-CDs binding hydrogen ion.

(A) The PL titration with introduction of Arg; (B) The relationship between (F0–F)/F0 and Arg content; (C) The linear relationship between (F0–F)/F0 versus Arg content; (D) Amino acids selectivity; (E) UV–Vis spectra of Arg, N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs/Arg; (F) Lifespan of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs/Arg.
CLSM images of HeLa cells treated with (a1-c1) pH = 4, (a2-c2) pH = 7, (a3-c3) pH = 10. Scale bar: 20 μm.
CLSM images of HeLa cells treated with (a1-c1) N,S-CDs, (a2-c2) N,S-CDs/Arg. Scale bar: 20 μm.
CLSM images of ZF hatched with (a1-c1) pH = 4, (a2-c2) pH = 7, (a3-c3) pH = 10.
CLSM images of ZF hatched with (a1-c1) N,S-CDs, (a2-c2) N,S-CDs/Arg.
The photographs of N,S-CDs/starch solid fluorescent powders under day light and UV light (365 nm).
The photographs of N,S-CDs/PVA fluorescent flexible film with various content of N,S-CDs under day light, UV light (254 nm) and UV light (365 nm).
Illustration for the synthetic procedure of N,S-CDs and their application for pH variation and arginine sensing in cellular and zebrafish.
Comparison of as-reported CDs for Arg sensing.

head.ce:title: Strategy to synthesize long-wavelength emission carbon dots and their multifunctional application for pH variation and arginine sensing and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, And School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: And School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong, 030600, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong 030600, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we designed N and S co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) with long-wavelength emission and their multifunctional application in pH variation, arginine (Arg) sensing, bioimaging in living cells and zebrafish, and fluorescent materials. The N,S-CDs with excitation wavelength-dependent properties were prepared using neutral red (NR) and dl-methionine (DL-Met) as raw materials by one-pot hydrothermal strategy. The N,S-CDs exhibited a unique pH-sensitive luminescence trait within pH range of 3.2–11.0 and have great linear relationship of 4.8–8.0, which indicating their potential application as an imaging reagent in physiological environments. Arg can quench the PL of N,S-CDs due to static quenching. (SQ). The linear range is 2.5–62.5 μM and the LOD is calculated as 0.68 μM. Furthermore, the as-proposed N,S-CDs can be applied as imaging reagents for monitoring of pH and Arg in vivo and vitro owing to outstanding biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Interestingly, the N,S-CDs were also used in fluorescent composite films and phosphors owing to exceptional optical properties. All these results indicate that the N,S-CDs have huge potentiality in the areas of fluorescence sensing, bioimaging and fluorescent materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as new generation of fluorescence nanomaterials, have been widely employed in fluorescence sensing [1–3], bioimaging [4–6], drug delivery [7–9] and other fields due to their excellent photostability [10–12], lower toxicity [13–15] and superior biocompatibility [16–18]. However, the emission of most prepared CDs so far was merely able to give rise to the blue and green emission region, which are hardly avoid the effect of autofluorescence in biosystem [19–23]. Therefore, it is indispensable to exploit preparation strategies for long-wavelength emission CDs (orange to red) [24–26].

Intracellular pH is closely related to the cell cycle and physiological and pathological processes such as apoptosis, receptor-mediated signal transduction, ion transport, muscle contraction, inflammation, and tumor growth [27–29]. Therefore, accurate determination of pH is of great significance for early diagnosis of diseases. Various alternative methods have been developed for detecting pH [30,31]. In particular, fluorescence methods have attracted attention due to their advantages of non-invasiveness, high signal-to-noise ratio, and higher sensitivity. Qu et al. [32] developed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor via blue-green dual-emitting CDs to determine temperature, pH, and Fe3+, which provided a novel strategy for determination of pH. Afterwards, some CDs-based pH fluorescent probes were also developed and exploited for pH sensing in living cells [33–38]. However, most of these pH fluorescent probes are on the basis of short-wavelength emission of fluorescent carbon dots, which is not auspicious to their further applications in biosystem. Therefore, there is a great need to develop CD-based pH detection methods with long-wavelength emission.

Arginine (Arg), as an indispensable amino acid, plays an eventful role in cell division, wound healing, excretion of ammonia, immune function, and secretion of hormones [39]. Abnormal levels of Arg may lead to cardiovascular diseases such as hypertension, diabetes, and atherosclerosis [40]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop feasible arginine assay for disease diagnosis. Meanwhile, in the food and beverage industry, the determination of Arg concentration is a key factor in monitoring product quality. Thence, it is essential to exploit a feasible arginine detection strategy. At present, the traditional detection methods of Arg are high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [41], electrochemical method [42], nuclear magnetic resonance method (NMR) [43,44], high temperature paper chromatography [45], and capillary electrophoresis [46]. However, these detection methods have the drawbacks of cumbersome operation, time-consuming, and need for sample pretreatment. The fluorescence method has attracted the attention of researchers owing to its benefits of simple operation, inexpensive expense and rapidity. Lu et al. [47] prepared CNDs-based fluorescent probes with blue emission for the detection of Arg and Cu2+ in cells, which unfolded a novel sight for Arg assay. Subsequently, some CDs-based Arg assays were also developed and used to detect Arg in real samples [48–51]. Unfortunately, these CDs-based fluorescent probes are located in the short-wavelength emission area, which greatly restricts their further applications in biosystem. Thence, it is an enormous challenge to exploit CD-based fluorescent probes of Arg with long-wavelength emission.

Herein, orange emission N,S-CDs were manufactured though hydrothermal process using neutral red (NR) and dl-methionine (DL-Met) as precursors and utilized for pH variation, arginine (Arg) sensing and bioimaging in HeLa cells and zebrafish (Scheme 1 ). N,S-CDs exhibited a unique pH-sensitive photoluminescence emission feature within pH range of 3.2–11.0, supplying a fluorescent probe for intracellular pH sensing. Besides, Arg can quench the orange luminescence of N,S-CDs via SQ. The linear range is 2.5–62.5 μM and LOD was 0.68 μM. The N,S-CDs have smoothly implemented pH fluctuation and Arg sensing in vivo and vitro owing to excellent biocompatibility and lower cytotoxicity. Interestingly, N,S-CDs/starch fluorescent powders and N,S-CDs/PVA fluorescent flexible films were prepared owing to the good photostability of N,S-CDs.

Typically, NR (5.9 mg) and DL-Met (11.8 mg) were synchronously placed in 20 mL deionized water. The mixture was poured into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. The N,S-CDs powder were obtained after filtration, dialysis, and lyophilization.

For monitoring of pH, 100 μL N,S-CDs (1.0 mg/mL) was mixed with 1.9 mL BR buffer solutions of various pH. Wait for 20 s and measure the fluorescence intensity. All experiments are repeated three times, and the measurement conditions are λex = 520 nm and λem = 625 nm.

To determine the PL-sensing properties of N,S-CDs towards Arg, 100 μL of N,S-CDs (1.0 mg/mL) was added to 1.9 mL of PBS solutions (pH 7.4), and then various contents of Arg were mixed to the N,S-CDs solutions. The PL intensity of the mixed solution was fathomed after 1 min.

HeLa cells were seeded in 15 mm dishes and hatched with N,S-CDs for 24h. Before imaging, cells were washed 3 times with PBS buffer pH = 7.4 and incubated in buffer solutions of different pH for 1 h. To detect Arg, cells were hatched with pH = 7.4 PBS buffer for 1 h and further incubated with Arg. Fluorescence images were obtained by LCSM.

In the early stage of the reaction, the hydrothermal temperature related to the fluorescence of N,S-CDs have been discussed in detail. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the PL Intensity of N,S-CDs reach to the optimal values under the reaction temperature of 180 °C for 6 h. The TEM (Fig. 1 A) displayed that the N,S-CDs were spherical with the even particle size of 2.48 nm (inset of Fig. 1A). The lattice fringe distance of 0.21 nm revealed by HRTEM, corresponding to the (100) facets of graphitic carbon.

In FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1B), it could be found that the surface of N,S-CDs had –NH/-OH (3409 cm−1), –CH (2917 cm−1), –SH (2917 cm−1), NN (2917 cm−1) and C–O/C–S (2917 cm−1) groups. As indicated in Fig. 1C, the full scale XPS pattern were ascribed to C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p, indicating that the N,S-CDs are comprised of C, N, O, and S. The C1s spectra of N,S-CDs (Fig. 1D) is decomposed into three peaks, including C–C/CC (284.8 eV), C–S (285.9 eV), C–N/C–O (287.9 eV) and COOH (288.9 eV). The splitting of the N1s peak (Fig. 1E), seen at 398.5 eV, 399.3 eV and 400.8 eV, could be assigned to pyridine N, amino N and pyrrole N, respectively. The novel peaks at 532.0 and 533.4 eV were attributed to CO and C–O bands in O1s spectrum (Fig. S2). Four peaks in the S2p spectrum (Fig. 1E) are assigned to S2P3/2 C–S–C (163.0 eV), S2P1/2 C–S–C (164.2 eV) and C-SOx- (165.4 and 166.5 eV). All these results indicated that N,S-CDs have hydrophilic group patterns such as amino groups.

The optical performances were determined through UV–vis and FL spectrum. The peaks at 275 nm was ascribed to the π-π* transition of the CC bond, and the bonds at 532 nm were originated from the macrocyclic aromatic conjugated structure of N,S-CDs (Fig. 2 A). The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of N,S-CDs were located at 527 and 623 nm, Moreover, the excitation-dependent PL behavior could be found in the 3D PL spectra (Fig. 2B). The CIE coordinate was calculated as (0.6506, 0.3490), which proved the latent use of N,S-CDs for biosensing (Fig. 2C). Rhodamine B was used as a reference, and the QY was 6.5% (Fig. S3). The optical stability was further explored by saline solution and xenon lamp irradiation. Fig. S4 illustrated that the PL intensity tended to be stable in 0.01–4 M sodium chloride solution.

The PL intensity of N,S-CDs gradually fallen with increasing pH from 3.2 to 11.0 (Fig. 3 A). The downward trend was fitted by the Boltzmann equation, and the fitting constant was 0.9985 (Fig. 3B). In the range of pH 4.8–8.0, pH and PL intensity demonstrated a good linearity (Fig. 3C, y = 6262–576.2x, R2 = 0.9888), which proving that N,S-CDs had the capacity to monitor pH changes within the physiologic extent. Besides, the photostability of N,S-CDs at several pH (4.0, 7.0, 10.0) was explored. Fig. S5 showed that the PL intensity of N,S-CDs was relatively stabilizing at different pH. More importantly, the response of N,S-CDs to pH was highly reversible. Through repeated adjustments of acid and alkali between pH 4.0 and 10.0, the PL intensity had not changed distinctly after 10 times, which certificating the excellent photostability of N,S-CDs at various pH (Fig. S6).

Subsequently, we explored the response mechanism of pH using fluorescence lifetime and zeta potential. The N1s spectrum displayed that the surface of N,S-CDs had three N-containing groups: pyridine N, amino N, and pyrrole N. Therefore, we conjectured that the response mechanism may be via the protonation and deprotonation of the N-containing groups on the surface of N,S-CDs. As the pH increased, the fluorescence lifetime gradually increased (Fig. S7), while the zeta potential gradually decreased (Fig. S8). As the concentration of H+ gradually decreases, the pyridine N on the surface of N,S-CDs was initially protonated and then the pyrrolic N was protonated owing to the acidity of pyrrole is greater than that of pyridine. The possibly mechanism of N,S-CDs to pH was the protonation and deprotonation of nitrogen-containing groups (Fig. 3D).

The UV–Vis spectra and PL lifetime were applied to demonstrate the photoluminescence quenching mechanism of Arg. After adding Arg, N,S-CDs appeared a novel peak at 459 nm, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching might be attributed to SQ (Fig. 4E). Addition of Arg had no influence on the PL lifetime of N,S-CDs (Fig. S11). Thence, the quenching mechanism of Arg is SQ. The SQ mechanism was further proved by the quenching rate constant Kq (Fig. S12). Kq = 4.8*1012 L/mol/s, much larger than 2*1010 L/mol/s.

To further illustrate the sensing performance of the as-fabricated N,S-CDs in complex environments, we used N,S-CDs as probes to detect Arg in human serum, human urine, and soybean. Table S1 shows that human serum and human urine samples do not contain Arg, while the content of Arg in soybean is 4.6 μM. The recoveries of the three samples in human serum, human urine, and soybean ranged from 85.6% to 105.9%, and the RSDs were all below 3.6% (n = 6).

To investigate the feasibility of N,S-CDs in biological system, HeLa cells are chosen as criteria for evaluating the cytotoxicity of neutral red and N,S-CDs. The cytotoxicity of N,S-CDs was evaluated by MTT (3-(4,5Dimethylthiazol-yl)-2,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5 diphenyltetrazolium bromide) method. As depicted in Fig. S13, the cell viability of N,S-CDs is higher than neutral red and reach to 80% while incubating HeLa cells with N,S-CDs from 0 to 250 μg mL−1, which indicates the superior biocompatibility and ultra-low cytotoxicity of the N,S-CDs.

For live cell imaging, HeLa cells were incubated with buffer pH = 4.0, 7.0 and 10.0 for 0.5 h. As shown in Fig. 5 , when pH = 4.0, bright orange fluorescence was emitted inside the cells. The intracellular fluorescence gradually weakened with increasing pH, which demonstrating the ability of N,S-CDs to determine pH fluctuations in vitro. For determination of Arg, HeLa cells were hatched with buffer (pH = 7.4) for 0.5 h, and then Arg was added and incubated with it for 30 min. As displayed in Fig. 6 , the intracellular PL intensity decreased significantly after adding Arg, which proved that N,S-CDs could monitor exogenous Arg in vitro.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent powder and fluorescent flexible film

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, long-wavelength emission N,S-CDs are designed for pH fluctuation, arginine sensing, bioimaging in vivo and vitro, and photovoltaic materials. The N,S-CDs were fabricated though hydrothermal treatment using neutral red and dl-methionine as raw materials. The N,S-CDs exhibited a unique pH-sensitive luminescence trait within pH range of 3.2–11.0 and have great linear relationship of 4.8–8.0. Besides, Arg can decrease the PL intensity of N,S-CDs on the basis of SQ. The N,S-CDs have been smoothly implemented for pH fluctuations and Arg sensing in vivo and vitro owing to outstanding biocompatibility and low toxicity. In addition, N,S-CDs can also be utilized to fabricate phosphors and fluorescent flexible films. The N,S-CDs are prospective long-wavelength nanomaterials for fluorescence sensing, bioimaging, and photovoltaic materials.

Yating Meng: Analyzed the data, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.Qiaozhi Guo: Investigation, Discussed the results.Hongmei Xu: Project administration, Formal analysis, Discussed the results.Yuan Jiao: Supervision.Yang Liu: Supervision.Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Supervision.Chuan Dong: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087, Grants 22104075), the Graduate Student Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (No. 2021Y036), the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province, China (No. 20210302124014) .

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2022.124180.
